question: 11. From any point \( (a, b) \) in the Cartesian plane, show that (i) three normals, real or imaginary, can be drawn to the parabola \( y^{2}=4 p x \); (ii) these are real and distinct if \( 4(2 p-a)^{3}+27 p b^{2}<0 \); (iii) two of them coincide if \( (a, b) \) lies on the curve \( 27 p y^{2}=4(x-2 p)^{3} \); (iv) all three coincide only if \( (a, b) \) is the point \( (2 p, 0) \).
solution:
Solution. The slope of the parabola at the point \( (x, y) \) is \( 2 p / y \). Hence the line joining \( (a, b) \) to \( (x, y) \) is normal to the parabola at \( (x, y) \) if and only if
\[
(y-b)=-\frac{y}{2 p}(x-a)=-\frac{y}{2 p}\left(\frac{y^{2}}{4 p}-a\right)
\]
(The case \( y=0 \) is also covered by this equation.) This reduces to
\[
y^{3}+4 p(2 p-a) y-8 p^{2} b=0
\]

This cubic will have three roots if counted with multiplicity, the roots may be real or complex. Since (real or complex) values of \( y \) correspond one to one with (real or imaginary) points on the parabola, part (i) of the problem has been established.

A real cubic equation of the form \( y^{3}+A y+B=0 \) will have all real roots if and only if \( \Delta=27 B^{2}+4 A^{3} \leq 0 \). Two roots will coincide if and only if \( \Delta=0 \), and all three roots will coincide if and only if \( A=B=0 \).

In the present case, \( A=4 p(2 p-a), B=-8 p^{2} b \). So \( \Delta=64 p^{3}[4(2 p \) \( \left.-a)^{3}+27 p b^{2}\right] \). The problem apparently intends that we take \( p>0 \) (as is usual with the standard form of the parabola). With this assumption, \( \Delta \) has the same sign as
\[
\Delta^{\prime}=4(2 p-a)^{3}+27 p b^{2}
\]

The roots of (1), and hence the points of the parabola, will be real and distinct if and only if \( \Delta^{\prime}<0 \). Two normals will coincide if and only if \( \Delta^{\prime}=0 \); that is, \( (a, b) \) lies on the curve \( 27 p y^{2}=4(x-2 p)^{3} \). Finally, all three normals will coincide if and only if \( 4 p(2 p-a)=0 \) and \( 8 p^{2} b=0 \), i.e., if and only if \( (a, b)=(2 p, 0) \).

The algebraic facts stated above for the roots of \( y^{3}+A y+B=0 \) can be derived by the methods of calculus as follows.

The graph of \( y^{3}+A y \) will be increasing with strictly positive slope if \( A>0 \), so any equation \( y^{3}+A y=-B \) will have just one real root and therefore two complex roots. Thus the roots are distinct if \( A>0 \).

If \( A=0 \), the equation will have one real and two complex roots for \( B \neq 0 \), and a triple root at 0 if \( B=0 \) also.

For \( A<0 \), the graph has turning points at
\[
\left(-\sqrt{-\frac{A}{3}},-\frac{2}{3} A \sqrt{-\frac{A}{3}}\right) \quad \text { and } \quad\left(+\sqrt{-\frac{A}{3}}, \frac{2}{3} A \sqrt{-\frac{A}{3}}\right) .
\]

It is then clear that the equation will have three distinct real roots if
\[
|B|<\left|\frac{2}{3} A \sqrt{-\frac{A}{3}}\right|,
\]
one double and one single root if
\[
|B|=\left|\frac{2}{3} A \sqrt{-\frac{A}{3}}\right|,
\]
and one real and two complex roots if
\[
|B|>\left|\frac{2}{3} A \sqrt{-\frac{A}{3}}\right| .
\]

Squaring these relations we can write them in terms of \( \Delta=27 B^{2}+4 A^{3} \), and we have
\[
\begin{aligned}
\Delta<0 \Rightarrow \text { three distinct real roots, } \\
\Delta=0 \Rightarrow \text { (at least) two equal real roots, } \\
\Delta>0 \Rightarrow \text { one real and two complex roots, } \\
A=B=0 \Leftrightarrow \text { a triple root. }
\end{aligned}
\]

These facts are often derived algebraically by showing that \( -\Delta \), which is called the discriminant of the cubic, is the product of the squares of the differences of the roots. See any text on the theory of equations.

descriptive_long.question: 11. From any point \( (pointax, pointay) \) in the Cartesian plane, show that (i) three normals, real or imaginary, can be drawn to the parabola \( ycoord^{2}=4 paraparam xcoord \); (ii) these are real and distinct if \( 4(2 paraparam-pointax)^{3}+27 paraparam pointay^{2}<0 \); (iii) two of them coincide if \( (pointax, pointay) \) lies on the curve \( 27 paraparam ycoord^{2}=4(xcoord-2 paraparam)^{3} \); (iv) all three coincide only if \( (pointax, pointay) \) is the point \( (2 paraparam, 0) \).
descriptive_long.solution:
Solution. The slope of the parabola at the point \( (xcoord, ycoord) \) is \( 2 paraparam / ycoord \). Hence the line joining \( (pointax, pointay) \) to \( (xcoord, ycoord) \) is normal to the parabola at \( (xcoord, ycoord) \) if and only if
\[
(ycoord-pointay)=-\frac{ycoord}{2 paraparam}(xcoord-pointax)=-\frac{ycoord}{2 paraparam}\left(\frac{ycoord^{2}}{4 paraparam}-pointax\right)
\]
(The case \( ycoord=0 \) is also covered by this equation.) This reduces to
\[
ycoord^{3}+4 paraparam(2 paraparam-pointax) ycoord-8 paraparam^{2} pointay=0
\]

This cubic will have three roots if counted with multiplicity, the roots may be real or complex. Since (real or complex) values of \( ycoord \) correspond one to one with (real or imaginary) points on the parabola, part (i) of the problem has been established.

A real cubic equation of the form \( ycoord^{3}+cubiccoef ycoord+cubiconst=0 \) will have all real roots if and only if \( discrim=27 cubiconst^{2}+4 cubiccoef^{3} \leq 0 \). Two roots will coincide if and only if \( discrim=0 \), and all three roots will coincide if and only if \( cubiccoef=cubiconst=0 \).

In the present case, \( cubiccoef=4 paraparam(2 paraparam-pointax),\; cubiconst=-8 paraparam^{2} pointay \). So \( discrim=64 paraparam^{3}[4(2 paraparam - pointax)^{3}+27 paraparam pointay^{2}] \). The problem apparently intends that we take \( paraparam>0 \) (as is usual with the standard form of the parabola). With this assumption, \( discrim \) has the same sign as
\[
discrim^{\prime}=4(2 paraparam-pointax)^{3}+27 paraparam pointay^{2}
\]

The roots of (1), and hence the points of the parabola, will be real and distinct if and only if \( discrim^{\prime}<0 \). Two normals will coincide if and only if \( discrim^{\prime}=0 \); that is, \( (pointax, pointay) \) lies on the curve \( 27 paraparam ycoord^{2}=4(xcoord-2 paraparam)^{3} \). Finally, all three normals will coincide if and only if \( 4 paraparam(2 paraparam-pointax)=0 \) and \( 8 paraparam^{2} pointay=0 \), i.e., if and only if \( (pointax, pointay)=(2 paraparam, 0) \).

The algebraic facts stated above for the roots of \( ycoord^{3}+cubiccoef ycoord+cubiconst=0 \) can be derived by the methods of calculus as follows.

The graph of \( ycoord^{3}+cubiccoef ycoord \) will be increasing with strictly positive slope if \( cubiccoef>0 \), so any equation \( ycoord^{3}+cubiccoef ycoord=-cubiconst \) will have just one real root and therefore two complex roots. Thus the roots are distinct if \( cubiccoef>0 \).

If \( cubiccoef=0 \), the equation will have one real and two complex roots for \( cubiconst \neq 0 \), and a triple root at 0 if \( cubiconst=0 \) also.

For \( cubiccoef<0 \), the graph has turning points at
\[
\left(-\sqrt{-\frac{cubiccoef}{3}},-\frac{2}{3} cubiccoef \sqrt{-\frac{cubiccoef}{3}}\right) \quad \text { and } \quad\left(+\sqrt{-\frac{cubiccoef}{3}}, \frac{2}{3} cubiccoef \sqrt{-\frac{cubiccoef}{3}}\right) .
\]

It is then clear that the equation will have three distinct real roots if
\[
|cubiconst|<\left|\frac{2}{3} cubiccoef \sqrt{-\frac{cubiccoef}{3}}\right|,
\]
one double and one single root if
\[
|cubiconst|=\left|\frac{2}{3} cubiccoef \sqrt{-\frac{cubiccoef}{3}}\right|,
\]
and one real and two complex roots if
\[
|cubiconst|>\left|\frac{2}{3} cubiccoef \sqrt{-\frac{cubiccoef}{3}}\right| .
\]

Squaring these relations we can write them in terms of \( discrim=27 cubiconst^{2}+4 cubiccoef^{3} \), and we have
\[
\begin{aligned}
discrim<0 \Rightarrow \text { three distinct real roots, } \\
discrim=0 \Rightarrow \text { (at least) two equal real roots, } \\
discrim>0 \Rightarrow \text { one real and two complex roots, } \\
cubiccoef=cubiconst=0 \Leftrightarrow \text { a triple root. }
\end{aligned}
\]

These facts are often derived algebraically by showing that \( -discrim \), which is called the discriminant of the cubic, is the product of the squares of the differences of the roots. See any text on the theory of equations.

descriptive_long_confusing.question: 11. From any point \( (sunflower, pinecones) \) in the Cartesian plane, show that (i) three normals, real or imaginary, can be drawn to the parabola \( dolphins^{2}=4 rainstorm lanterns \); (ii) these are real and distinct if \( 4(2 rainstorm-sunflower)^{3}+27 rainstorm pinecones^{2}<0 \); (iii) two of them coincide if \( (sunflower, pinecones) \) lies on the curve \( 27 rainstorm dolphins^{2}=4(lanterns-2 rainstorm)^{3} \); (iv) all three coincide only if \( (sunflower, pinecones) \) is the point \( (2 rainstorm, 0) \).
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
Solution. The slope of the parabola at the point \( (lanterns, dolphins) \) is \( 2 rainstorm / dolphins \). Hence the line joining \( (sunflower, pinecones) \) to \( (lanterns, dolphins) \) is normal to the parabola at \( (lanterns, dolphins) \) if and only if
\[
(dolphins-pinecones)=-\frac{dolphins}{2 rainstorm}(lanterns-sunflower)=-\frac{dolphins}{2 rainstorm}\left(\frac{dolphins^{2}}{4 rainstorm}-sunflower\right)
\]
(The case \( dolphins=0 \) is also covered by this equation.) This reduces to
\[
dolphins^{3}+4 rainstorm(2 rainstorm-sunflower) dolphins-8 rainstorm^{2} pinecones=0
\]

This cubic will have three roots if counted with multiplicity, the roots may be real or complex. Since (real or complex) values of \( dolphins \) correspond one to one with (real or imaginary) points on the parabola, part (i) of the problem has been established.

A real cubic equation of the form \( dolphins^{3}+orchards \, dolphins+meanders=0 \) will have all real roots if and only if \( galaxies=27 \, meanders^{2}+4 \, orchards^{3} \leq 0 \). Two roots will coincide if and only if \( galaxies=0 \), and all three roots will coincide if and only if \( orchards=meanders=0 \).

In the present case, \( orchards=4 rainstorm(2 rainstorm-sunflower), \; meanders=-8 rainstorm^{2} pinecones \). So \( galaxies=64 rainstorm^{3}[4(2 rainstorm-sunflower)^{3}+27 rainstorm \, pinecones^{2}] \). The problem apparently intends that we take \( rainstorm>0 \) (as is usual with the standard form of the parabola). With this assumption, \( galaxies \) has the same sign as
\[
\galaxies^{\prime}=4(2 rainstorm-sunflower)^{3}+27 rainstorm \, pinecones^{2}
\]

The roots of (1), and hence the points of the parabola, will be real and distinct if and only if \( galaxies^{\prime}<0 \). Two normals will coincide if and only if \( galaxies^{\prime}=0 \); that is, \( (sunflower, pinecones) \) lies on the curve \( 27 rainstorm dolphins^{2}=4(lanterns-2 rainstorm)^{3} \). Finally, all three normals will coincide if and only if \( 4 rainstorm(2 rainstorm-sunflower)=0 \) and \( 8 rainstorm^{2} pinecones=0 \), i.e., if and only if \( (sunflower, pinecones)=(2 rainstorm, 0) \).

The algebraic facts stated above for the roots of \( dolphins^{3}+orchards \, dolphins+meanders=0 \) can be derived by the methods of calculus as follows.

The graph of \( dolphins^{3}+orchards \, dolphins \) will be increasing with strictly positive slope if \( orchards>0 \), so any equation \( dolphins^{3}+orchards \, dolphins=-meanders \) will have just one real root and therefore two complex roots. Thus the roots are distinct if \( orchards>0 \).

If \( orchards=0 \), the equation will have one real and two complex roots for \( meanders \neq 0 \), and a triple root at 0 if \( meanders=0 \) also.

For \( orchards<0 \), the graph has turning points at
\[
\left(-\sqrt{-\frac{orchards}{3}},-\frac{2}{3} \, orchards \sqrt{-\frac{orchards}{3}}\right) \quad \text { and } \quad\left(+\sqrt{-\frac{orchards}{3}}, \frac{2}{3} \, orchards \sqrt{-\frac{orchards}{3}}\right) .
\]

It is then clear that the equation will have three distinct real roots if
\[
|meanders|<\left|\frac{2}{3} \, orchards \sqrt{-\frac{orchards}{3}}\right|,
\]
one double and one single root if
\[
|meanders|=\left|\frac{2}{3} \, orchards \sqrt{-\frac{orchards}{3}}\right|,
\]
and one real and two complex roots if
\[
|meanders|>\left|\frac{2}{3} \, orchards \sqrt{-\frac{orchards}{3}}\right| .
\]

Squaring these relations we can write them in terms of \( galaxies=27 \, meanders^{2}+4 \, orchards^{3} \), and we have
\[
\begin{aligned}
\galaxies<0 &\Rightarrow \text { three distinct real roots, } \\
\galaxies=0 &\Rightarrow \text { (at least) two equal real roots, } \\
\galaxies>0 &\Rightarrow \text { one real and two complex roots, } \\
orchards=meanders=0 &\Leftrightarrow \text { a triple root. }
\end{aligned}
\]

These facts are often derived algebraically by showing that \( -\galaxies \), which is called the discriminant of the cubic, is the product of the squares of the differences of the roots. See any text on the theory of equations.

descriptive_long_misleading.question: From any point \( (terminalvalue, centralvalue) \) in the Cartesian plane, show that (i) three normals, real or imaginary, can be drawn to the parabola \( horizontalaxis^{2}=4 randomvariable verticalaxis \); (ii) these are real and distinct if \( 4(2 randomvariable-terminalvalue)^{3}+27 randomvariable centralvalue^{2}<0 \); (iii) two of them coincide if \( (terminalvalue, centralvalue) \) lies on the curve \( 27 randomvariable horizontalaxis^{2}=4(verticalaxis-2 randomvariable)^{3} \); (iv) all three coincide only if \( (terminalvalue, centralvalue) \) is the point \( (2 randomvariable, 0) \).
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
Solution. The slope of the parabola at the point \( (verticalaxis, horizontalaxis) \) is \( 2 randomvariable / horizontalaxis \). Hence the line joining \( (terminalvalue, centralvalue) \) to \( (verticalaxis, horizontalaxis) \) is normal to the parabola at \( (verticalaxis, horizontalaxis) \) if and only if
\[
(horizontalaxis-centralvalue)=-\frac{horizontalaxis}{2 randomvariable}(verticalaxis-terminalvalue)=-\frac{horizontalaxis}{2 randomvariable}\left(\frac{horizontalaxis^{2}}{4 randomvariable}-terminalvalue\right)
\]
(The case \( horizontalaxis=0 \) is also covered by this equation.) This reduces to
\[
horizontalaxis^{3}+4 randomvariable(2 randomvariable-terminalvalue) horizontalaxis-8 randomvariable^{2} centralvalue=0
\]

This cubic will have three roots if counted with multiplicity, the roots may be real or complex. Since (real or complex) values of \( horizontalaxis \) correspond one to one with (real or imaginary) points on the parabola, part (i) of the problem has been established.

A real cubic equation of the form \( horizontalaxis^{3}+negativefactor horizontalaxis+variablefactor=0 \) will have all real roots if and only if \( equilibrium=27 variablefactor^{2}+4 negativefactor^{3} \leq 0 \). Two roots will coincide if and only if \( equilibrium=0 \), and all three roots will coincide if and only if \( negativefactor=variablefactor=0 \).

In the present case, \( negativefactor=4 randomvariable(2 randomvariable-terminalvalue),\; variablefactor=-8 randomvariable^{2} centralvalue \). So \( equilibrium=64 randomvariable^{3}[4(2 randomvariable \) \( \left.-terminalvalue)^{3}+27 randomvariable centralvalue^{2}\right] \). The problem apparently intends that we take \( randomvariable>0 \) (as is usual with the standard form of the parabola). With this assumption, \( equilibrium \) has the same sign as
\[
equilibrium^{\prime}=4(2 randomvariable-terminalvalue)^{3}+27 randomvariable centralvalue^{2}
\]

The roots of (1), and hence the points of the parabola, will be real and distinct if and only if \( equilibrium^{\prime}<0 \). Two normals will coincide if and only if \( equilibrium^{\prime}=0 \); that is, \( (terminalvalue, centralvalue) \) lies on the curve \( 27 randomvariable horizontalaxis^{2}=4(verticalaxis-2 randomvariable)^{3} \). Finally, all three normals will coincide if and only if \( 4 randomvariable(2 randomvariable-terminalvalue)=0 \) and \( 8 randomvariable^{2} centralvalue=0 \), i.e., if and only if \( (terminalvalue, centralvalue)=(2 randomvariable, 0) \).

The algebraic facts stated above for the roots of \( horizontalaxis^{3}+negativefactor horizontalaxis+variablefactor=0 \) can be derived by the methods of calculus as follows.

The graph of \( horizontalaxis^{3}+negativefactor horizontalaxis \) will be increasing with strictly positive slope if \( negativefactor>0 \), so any equation \( horizontalaxis^{3}+negativefactor horizontalaxis=-variablefactor \) will have just one real root and therefore two complex roots. Thus the roots are distinct if \( negativefactor>0 \).

If \( negativefactor=0 \), the equation will have one real and two complex roots for \( variablefactor \neq 0 \), and a triple root at 0 if \( variablefactor=0 \) also.

For \( negativefactor<0 \), the graph has turning points at
\[
\left(-\sqrt{-\frac{negativefactor}{3}},-\frac{2}{3} negativefactor \sqrt{-\frac{negativefactor}{3}}\right) \quad \text { and } \quad\left(+\sqrt{-\frac{negativefactor}{3}}, \frac{2}{3} negativefactor \sqrt{-\frac{negativefactor}{3}}\right) .
\]

It is then clear that the equation will have three distinct real roots if
\[
|variablefactor|<\left|\frac{2}{3} negativefactor \sqrt{-\frac{negativefactor}{3}}\right|,
\]
one double and one single root if
\[
|variablefactor|=\left|\frac{2}{3} negativefactor \sqrt{-\frac{negativefactor}{3}}\right|,
\]
and one real and two complex roots if
\[
|variablefactor|>\left|\frac{2}{3} negativefactor \sqrt{-\frac{negativefactor}{3}}\right| .
\]

Squaring these relations we can write them in terms of \( equilibrium=27 variablefactor^{2}+4 negativefactor^{3} \), and we have
\[
\begin{aligned}
equilibrium<0 \Rightarrow \text { three distinct real roots, } \\
equilibrium=0 \Rightarrow \text { (at least) two equal real roots, } \\
equilibrium>0 \Rightarrow \text { one real and two complex roots, } \\
negativefactor=variablefactor=0 \Leftrightarrow \text { a triple root. }
\end{aligned}
\]

These facts are often derived algebraically by showing that \( -equilibrium \), which is called the discriminant of the cubic, is the product of the squares of the differences of the roots. See any text on the theory of equations.

garbled_string.question: 11. From any point \( (fnobrikc, telawmzi) \) in the Cartesian plane, show that (i) three normals, real or imaginary, can be drawn to the parabola \( hjgrksla^{2}=4 goshtvra qzxwvtnp \); (ii) these are real and distinct if \( 4(2 goshtvra-fnobrikc)^{3}+27 goshtvra telawmzi^{2}<0 \); (iii) two of them coincide if \( (fnobrikc, telawmzi) \) lies on the curve \( 27 goshtvra hjgrksla^{2}=4(qzxwvtnp-2 goshtvra)^{3} \); (iv) all three coincide only if \( (fnobrikc, telawmzi) \) is the point \( (2 goshtvra, 0) \).
garbled_string.solution:
Solution. The slope of the parabola at the point \( (qzxwvtnp, hjgrksla) \) is \( 2 goshtvra / hjgrksla \). Hence the line joining \( (fnobrikc, telawmzi) \) to \( (qzxwvtnp, hjgrksla) \) is normal to the parabola at \( (qzxwvtnp, hjgrksla) \) if and only if
\[
(hjgrksla-telawmzi)=-\frac{hjgrksla}{2 goshtvra}(qzxwvtnp-fnobrikc)=-\frac{hjgrksla}{2 goshtvra}\left(\frac{hjgrksla^{2}}{4 goshtvra}-fnobrikc\right)
\]
(The case \( hjgrksla=0 \) is also covered by this equation.) This reduces to
\[
hjgrksla^{3}+4 goshtvra(2 goshtvra-fnobrikc) hjgrksla-8 goshtvra^{2} telawmzi=0
\]

This cubic will have three roots if counted with multiplicity, the roots may be real or complex. Since (real or complex) values of \( hjgrksla \) correspond one to one with (real or imaginary) points on the parabola, part (i) of the problem has been established.

A real cubic equation of the form \( hjgrksla^{3}+mpldqezi hjgrksla+vstokhya=0 \) will have all real roots if and only if \( rcqusbne=27 vstokhya^{2}+4 mpldqezi^{3} \leq 0 \). Two roots will coincide if and only if \( rcqusbne=0 \), and all three roots will coincide if and only if \( mpldqezi=vstokhya=0 \).

In the present case, \( mpldqezi=4 goshtvra(2 goshtvra-fnobrikc), \; vstokhya=-8 goshtvra^{2} telawmzi \). So \( rcqusbne=64 goshtvra^{3}[4(2 goshtvra-fnobrikc)^{3}+27 goshtvra telawmzi^{2}] \). The problem apparently intends that we take \( goshtvra>0 \) (as is usual with the standard form of the parabola). With this assumption, \( rcqusbne \) has the same sign as
\[
rcqusbne^{\prime}=4(2 goshtvra-fnobrikc)^{3}+27 goshtvra telawmzi^{2}
\]

The roots of (1), and hence the points of the parabola, will be real and distinct if and only if \( rcqusbne^{\prime}<0 \). Two normals will coincide if and only if \( rcqusbne^{\prime}=0 \); that is, \( (fnobrikc, telawmzi) \) lies on the curve \( 27 goshtvra hjgrksla^{2}=4(qzxwvtnp-2 goshtvra)^{3} \). Finally, all three normals will coincide if and only if \( 4 goshtvra(2 goshtvra-fnobrikc)=0 \) and \( 8 goshtvra^{2} telawmzi=0 \), i.e., if and only if \( (fnobrikc, telawmzi)=(2 goshtvra, 0) \).

The algebraic facts stated above for the roots of \( hjgrksla^{3}+mpldqezi hjgrksla+vstokhya=0 \) can be derived by the methods of calculus as follows.

The graph of \( hjgrksla^{3}+mpldqezi hjgrksla \) will be increasing with strictly positive slope if \( mpldqezi>0 \), so any equation \( hjgrksla^{3}+mpldqezi hjgrksla=-vstokhya \) will have just one real root and therefore two complex roots. Thus the roots are distinct if \( mpldqezi>0 \).

If \( mpldqezi=0 \), the equation will have one real and two complex roots for \( vstokhya \neq 0 \), and a triple root at 0 if \( vstokhya=0 \) also.

For \( mpldqezi<0 \), the graph has turning points at
\[
\left(-\sqrt{-\frac{mpldqezi}{3}},-\frac{2}{3} mpldqezi \sqrt{-\frac{mpldqezi}{3}}\right) \quad \text { and } \quad\left(+\sqrt{-\frac{mpldqezi}{3}}, \frac{2}{3} mpldqezi \sqrt{-\frac{mpldqezi}{3}}\right) .
\]

It is then clear that the equation will have three distinct real roots if
\[
|vstokhya|<\left|\frac{2}{3} mpldqezi \sqrt{-\frac{mpldqezi}{3}}\right|,
\]
one double and one single root if
\[
|vstokhya|=\left|\frac{2}{3} mpldqezi \sqrt{-\frac{mpldqezi}{3}}\right|,
\]
and one real and two complex roots if
\[
|vstokhya|>\left|\frac{2}{3} mpldqezi \sqrt{-\frac{mpldqezi}{3}}\right| .
\]

Squaring these relations we can write them in terms of \( rcqusbne=27 vstokhya^{2}+4 mpldqezi^{3} \), and we have
\[
\begin{aligned}
rcqusbne<0 &\Rightarrow \text { three distinct real roots, } \\
rcqusbne=0 &\Rightarrow \text { (at least) two equal real roots, } \\
rcqusbne>0 &\Rightarrow \text { one real and two complex roots, } \\
mpldqezi=vstokhya=0 &\Leftrightarrow \text { a triple root. }
\end{aligned}
\]

These facts are often derived algebraically by showing that \( -rcqusbne \), which is called the discriminant of the cubic, is the product of the squares of the differences of the roots. See any text on the theory of equations.

kernel_variant.question:
Fix a negative real constant c and consider the left-opening parabola  

  \Gamma  \subset  \mathbb{R}^2 :  y^2 = 4 c x   (c < 0).  

For an arbitrary point P =(u , v) \in  \mathbb{R}^2 investigate the (real or complex) straight lines that are normal to \Gamma  and pass through P.
Put  

  \Delta (P) = 4(2c - u)^3 + 27 c v^2                (the \Delta -function)  

and keep the notation throughout.

Five assertions lead from the elementary ``three-normals'' fact to a rich
picture involving projective duality, evolutes, singularity theory and small
analytic perturbations.

(i) (Existence in the complex projective line)  
Show that, counted with multiplicity, exactly three complex normal lines pass through P; equivalently, prove that the feet \zeta  of those normals are precisely the three (possibly coincident, possibly non-real) roots of  

  \zeta ^3 + 4 c(2c - u) \zeta  - 8 c^2 v = 0.         (\star )

(ii) (Evolute of the parabola)  
Prove that the cubic curve  

  H : 27 c y^2 = 4(x - 2c)^3             ()

is the evolute of \Gamma , i.e. the locus of the centres of curvature of \Gamma .  
In particular, show that for every Q \in  H there exists a unique circle having third-order contact with \Gamma  and whose centre is Q.  
Check explicitly that the cusp of H is the point (2c,0).

(iii) (Real analytic stratification and an algebraic area invariant)  
(a) Let D(P) be the discriminant of (\star ).  Show that  

  D(P) = -64 c^3 \Delta (P)

and hence, because c < 0, D(P) and \Delta (P) have the same sign.  Conclude the
reality pattern of the normals:

  \Delta (P) > 0 \Leftrightarrow  three distinct real normals,  
  \Delta (P) = 0 \Leftrightarrow  at least two coincident real normals,  
  \Delta (P) < 0 \Leftrightarrow  exactly one real normal.

(b) Let Y_1,Y_2,Y_3 be the (complex) feet of the three normals from P (counting multiplicities) and set  

  A(P) = \frac{1}{2} det
  1 x_1 y_1
   1 x_2 y_2
   1 x_3 y_3            (\dagger )

(the oriented area of the triangle determined by the feet).  
Prove the identity  

  A(P)^2 = - c \Delta (P).            (\ddagger )

In particular, \Delta (P)=0 \Leftrightarrow  A(P)=0 \Leftrightarrow  the three feet are collinear \Leftrightarrow  P \in  H.

(iv) (Projective dual curve of \Gamma )  
Embed the discussion in the complex projective plane \mathbb{P}^2(\mathbb{C}).  
Show that the set \Gamma * of all (complex) normal lines to \Gamma  is the rational cuspidal cubic  

  c s^3 + 2 c s t^2 + u t^2 = 0    in dual (Plucker) coordinates [s:t:u]\neq 0,  (\Delta )

and that the naturally induced normal map  

  \nu  : \Gamma  \to  \Gamma *, X \mapsto  normal line at X,

has degree three.  Consequently, every line of \Gamma * not passing through the cusp [0:0:1] is normal to \Gamma  at three (possibly coincident) points.

(v) (Stability under analytic perturbations)  
Let f be a real analytic function with f(0)=f'(0)=0 and consider the \varepsilon -perturbed parabola  

  \Gamma _\varepsilon  : y^2 = 4c x + \varepsilon  f(x)  (\varepsilon  \in  \mathbb{R}, |\varepsilon | << 1).

Fix P\in \mathbb{R}^2 and keep \Delta (P) as above.

(a) If \Delta (P) \neq  0 (i.e. P \notin  H) prove that there exists \varepsilon _0>0 such that for |\varepsilon |<\varepsilon _0 the equation of the normals to \Gamma _\varepsilon  through P still possesses exactly three distinct complex solutions; the reality pattern is unchanged.

(b) If \Delta (P)=0 but P \neq  (2c,0) (P a regular point of H) show that the triple of normals splits analytically: there is an explicit real-analytic function g(P) (computed in the solution) such that  

  \Delta _\varepsilon (P) = \Delta (P) + \varepsilon  g(P) + O(\varepsilon ^2) = \varepsilon  g(P) + O(\varepsilon ^2).

For \varepsilon  g(P)>0 the three perturbed normals are real and distinct, whereas for \varepsilon  g(P)<0 only one real normal survives.

(c) Analyse separately the cusp P_0=(2c,0): prove that \Delta _\varepsilon (P_0)=O(\varepsilon ^2) and describe the local unfolding of the fivefold contact between \Gamma  and its evolute at P_0.
kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout c<0 is fixed and

  X(y) = (y^2/(4c) , y)  (y \in  \mathbb{C})

parametrises \Gamma .

(i) Number of complex normals  
The tangent vector at X(y) is T(y) = (y/(2c) , 1).
A line through P = (u,v) is normal at X(y) iff (P-X(y))\cdot T(y)=0, i.e.  

  (u - y^2/4c)(y/2c) + (v - y) = 0  
 \Leftrightarrow  y^3 + 4 c(2c - u) y - 8 c^2 v = 0,  

which is exactly (\star ).  As the cubic is monic it has three roots
\zeta _1,\zeta _2,\zeta _3 in \mathbb{C}, counted with multiplicity, and each root produces one normal line through P. Hence exactly three complex normals exist.

(ii) The evolute  
1. Curvature and normal.  With parameter y one has

 x' = y/(2c), y' = 1, x'' = 1/(2c), y'' = 0,

so the curvature is

 \kappa (y)= |x'y''-y'x''| / (x'^2+y'^2)^{3/2}
    = (1/(2|c|)) \cdot  (8|c|^3)/(y^2+4c^2)^{3/2}
    = 4|c|^2 /(y^2+4c^2)^{3/2}.                     (1)

The unit normal (obtained by rotating the unit tangent through +\pi /2) is  

 n(y)= ( 2c , -y ) / \sqrt{y^2+4c^2}.                   (2)

2. Centre of curvature.  
Its position vector is

 C(y)=X(y)+\kappa (y)^{-1} n(y)
    =(y^2/(4c)+ (y^2+4c^2)/(2c), y-y(y^2+4c^2)/(4c^2))             (3)
    =( 2c + 3y^2/(4c) ,  -y^3/(4c^2) ).                          (4)

3. Elimination of y.  
Setting (x,y) = C(y) and eliminating y between (4) gives

 27 c y^2 = 4(x - 2c)^3,

which is ().  Each y corresponds to one point of H and vice-versa, hence H is the evolute of \Gamma .  By construction the circle with centre C(y) and radius \rho (y)=1/\kappa (y) satisfies third-order contact with \Gamma  at X(y) and is unique.

(iii) Reality pattern and the algebraic area invariant  

(a) Reality.  
Write (\star ) as Y^3 + A Y + B = 0 with

 A = 4c(2c - u), B = -8c^2 v.

The discriminant of a depressed cubic is

 D = -4A^3 - 27B^2 = -64 c^3 \Delta (P).                     (5)

Because c<0, the factor -64 c^3 is positive, hence sign(D)=sign(\Delta ).  
For a monic cubic:

 D>0  \Leftrightarrow  three distinct real roots,  
 D=0  \Leftrightarrow  at least two coincident real roots,  
 D<0  \Leftrightarrow  exactly one real root.

This gives the announced classification in terms of \Delta (P).

(b) Algebraic area.  
Let y_1,y_2,y_3 be the roots of (\star ).
From Vieta,

 y_1+y_2+y_3 = 0, 
 y_1y_2+y_2y_3+y_3y_1 = A, 
 y_1y_2y_3 = -B.                                      (6)

Put x_i = y_i^2/(4c).  As in the original computation

 det(1,x_i,y_i) = -(y_1-y_2)(y_2-y_3)(y_3-y_1)/(4c).    (7)

Hence

 A(P)^2 = 1/(64 c^2) \cdot  (y_1-y_2)^2(y_2-y_3)^2(y_3-y_1)^2
        = D /(64 c^2)  (using (5))                  (8)
        = - c \Delta (P).                                (\ddagger )

Because -c > 0, \Delta (P)=0 \Leftrightarrow  A(P)=0, so the three feet are collinear
exactly when P lies on the evolute H.

(iv) The projective dual (normal) curve \Gamma *  

1. Plucker coordinates of a normal.  
The affine normal at X(y) has equation

 (y - y) = -y/(2c) (x - y^2/4c)
 \Leftrightarrow  (y/(2c)) x - y + (-y - y^3/(8c^2)) = 0.

Up to scale the Plucker coordinates are therefore  

 [s : t : u] = [ y : 2c : -2c y - y^3/(4c) ].          (9)

2. Eliminating y gives

 c s^3 + 2c s t^2 + u t^2 = 0,                          (\Delta )

an irreducible rational cubic with a unique cusp at [0:0:1].  
Hence \Gamma * is rational and cuspidal; the map y \mapsto  (9) has degree three, so
a generic line of \Gamma * is normal to \Gamma  at three points.

(v) Analytic stability under small perturbations  

Let F_\varepsilon (x,y)=y^2-4c x-\varepsilon  f(x).  A point (x,y) lies on \Gamma _\varepsilon \Leftrightarrow F_\varepsilon =0, and
\nabla F_\varepsilon =(-4c-\varepsilon  f'(x), 2y).
Repeating the calculation in (i) with these quantities produces

 y^3 + 4c(2c - u) y - 8c^2 v + \varepsilon  G_1(y;P) + \varepsilon ^2 G_2(y;P,\varepsilon ) = 0,   (\star _\varepsilon )

where G_1 is a polynomial of degree \leq 2 in y whose coefficients are
real-analytic in P and depend on f,f',f''.  The discriminant of (\star _\varepsilon )
is an analytic function of \varepsilon :

 Disc_\varepsilon (P) = \Delta (P) + \varepsilon  g(P) + O(\varepsilon ^2).                (10)

A direct differentiation of the classical discriminant formula yields

 g(P)= -32 c (2c-u) f'(u) - 27 c v^2 f''(u).           (11)

(a) If \Delta (P)\neq 0.  
Then Disc_\varepsilon (P)=\Delta (P)+O(\varepsilon ) never vanishes for |\varepsilon |<\varepsilon _0 small, so the
three normals remain simple; their real/complex nature does not change.

(b) If \Delta (P)=0 and P\neq (2c,0).  
Now Disc_\varepsilon (P)=\varepsilon  g(P)+O(\varepsilon ^2).  For g(P)\neq 0 the sign of \varepsilon  g(P) decides:

 \varepsilon  g(P) > 0 \Leftrightarrow  Disc_\varepsilon  > 0 \Leftrightarrow  three distinct real normals,  
 \varepsilon  g(P) < 0 \Leftrightarrow  Disc_\varepsilon  < 0 \Leftrightarrow  only one real normal.

(c) The cusp P_0=(2c,0).  
Here y=0 is a triple root of the unperturbed cubic; a short computation
shows Disc_\varepsilon (P_0)=O(\varepsilon ^2).  Under a generic perturbation \Gamma  and its evolute
split into a 1+2 configuration, modelling the A_2-singularity.

Thus each item of the corrected statement is proved.

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
• Part (i) is essentially the original statement but couched in projective-complex language to force the solver to manipulate homogeneous objects and multiplicities.  

• Part (ii) introduces differential geometry (curvature, evolute, osculating circle) and demands non-trivial elimination to obtain equation (♣).  

• Part (iii) links classical discriminant theory with a geometric invariant (oriented area), requiring skill in both algebraic symmetrics and planar geometry.  

• Part (iv) moves the problem into the dual projective plane, requiring knowledge of Plücker coordinates, rational parametrisations, and singularity analysis of the cuspidal cubic.  

• Part (v) adds an analytic-perturbation layer: the solver must produce a stability argument via the Implicit-Function Theorem and compute the first variation of the discriminant.

All these layers greatly exceed the scope of the original exercise, demand several advanced techniques (differential geometry, algebraic geometry, singularity theory, real analytic perturbation), and intertwine them in a single coherent problem, making the enhanced variant significantly harder than both the original and the current kernel variant.

original_kernel_variant.question:
Fix a negative real constant c and consider the left-opening parabola  

  \Gamma  \subset  \mathbb{R}^2 :  y^2 = 4 c x   (c < 0).  

For an arbitrary point P =(u , v) \in  \mathbb{R}^2 investigate the (real or complex) straight lines that are normal to \Gamma  and pass through P.
Put  

  \Delta (P) = 4(2c - u)^3 + 27 c v^2                (the \Delta -function)  

and keep the notation throughout.

Five assertions lead from the elementary ``three-normals'' fact to a rich
picture involving projective duality, evolutes, singularity theory and small
analytic perturbations.

(i) (Existence in the complex projective line)  
Show that, counted with multiplicity, exactly three complex normal lines pass through P; equivalently, prove that the feet \zeta  of those normals are precisely the three (possibly coincident, possibly non-real) roots of  

  \zeta ^3 + 4 c(2c - u) \zeta  - 8 c^2 v = 0.         (\star )

(ii) (Evolute of the parabola)  
Prove that the cubic curve  

  H : 27 c y^2 = 4(x - 2c)^3             ()

is the evolute of \Gamma , i.e. the locus of the centres of curvature of \Gamma .  
In particular, show that for every Q \in  H there exists a unique circle having third-order contact with \Gamma  and whose centre is Q.  
Check explicitly that the cusp of H is the point (2c,0).

(iii) (Real analytic stratification and an algebraic area invariant)  
(a) Let D(P) be the discriminant of (\star ).  Show that  

  D(P) = -64 c^3 \Delta (P)

and hence, because c < 0, D(P) and \Delta (P) have the same sign.  Conclude the
reality pattern of the normals:

  \Delta (P) > 0 \Leftrightarrow  three distinct real normals,  
  \Delta (P) = 0 \Leftrightarrow  at least two coincident real normals,  
  \Delta (P) < 0 \Leftrightarrow  exactly one real normal.

(b) Let Y_1,Y_2,Y_3 be the (complex) feet of the three normals from P (counting multiplicities) and set  

  A(P) = \frac{1}{2} det
  1 x_1 y_1
   1 x_2 y_2
   1 x_3 y_3            (\dagger )

(the oriented area of the triangle determined by the feet).  
Prove the identity  

  A(P)^2 = - c \Delta (P).            (\ddagger )

In particular, \Delta (P)=0 \Leftrightarrow  A(P)=0 \Leftrightarrow  the three feet are collinear \Leftrightarrow  P \in  H.

(iv) (Projective dual curve of \Gamma )  
Embed the discussion in the complex projective plane \mathbb{P}^2(\mathbb{C}).  
Show that the set \Gamma * of all (complex) normal lines to \Gamma  is the rational cuspidal cubic  

  c s^3 + 2 c s t^2 + u t^2 = 0    in dual (Plucker) coordinates [s:t:u]\neq 0,  (\Delta )

and that the naturally induced normal map  

  \nu  : \Gamma  \to  \Gamma *, X \mapsto  normal line at X,

has degree three.  Consequently, every line of \Gamma * not passing through the cusp [0:0:1] is normal to \Gamma  at three (possibly coincident) points.

(v) (Stability under analytic perturbations)  
Let f be a real analytic function with f(0)=f'(0)=0 and consider the \varepsilon -perturbed parabola  

  \Gamma _\varepsilon  : y^2 = 4c x + \varepsilon  f(x)  (\varepsilon  \in  \mathbb{R}, |\varepsilon | << 1).

Fix P\in \mathbb{R}^2 and keep \Delta (P) as above.

(a) If \Delta (P) \neq  0 (i.e. P \notin  H) prove that there exists \varepsilon _0>0 such that for |\varepsilon |<\varepsilon _0 the equation of the normals to \Gamma _\varepsilon  through P still possesses exactly three distinct complex solutions; the reality pattern is unchanged.

(b) If \Delta (P)=0 but P \neq  (2c,0) (P a regular point of H) show that the triple of normals splits analytically: there is an explicit real-analytic function g(P) (computed in the solution) such that  

  \Delta _\varepsilon (P) = \Delta (P) + \varepsilon  g(P) + O(\varepsilon ^2) = \varepsilon  g(P) + O(\varepsilon ^2).

For \varepsilon  g(P)>0 the three perturbed normals are real and distinct, whereas for \varepsilon  g(P)<0 only one real normal survives.

(c) Analyse separately the cusp P_0=(2c,0): prove that \Delta _\varepsilon (P_0)=O(\varepsilon ^2) and describe the local unfolding of the fivefold contact between \Gamma  and its evolute at P_0.

Throughout c<0 is fixed and

  X(y) = (y^2/(4c) , y)  (y \in  \mathbb{C})

parametrises \Gamma .

(i) Number of complex normals  
The tangent vector at X(y) is T(y) = (y/(2c) , 1).
A line through P = (u,v) is normal at X(y) iff (P-X(y))\cdot T(y)=0, i.e.  

  (u - y^2/4c)(y/2c) + (v - y) = 0  
 \Leftrightarrow  y^3 + 4 c(2c - u) y - 8 c^2 v = 0,  

which is exactly (\star ).  As the cubic is monic it has three roots
\zeta _1,\zeta _2,\zeta _3 in \mathbb{C}, counted with multiplicity, and each root produces one normal line through P. Hence exactly three complex normals exist.

(ii) The evolute  
1. Curvature and normal.  With parameter y one has

 x' = y/(2c), y' = 1, x'' = 1/(2c), y'' = 0,

so the curvature is

 \kappa (y)= |x'y''-y'x''| / (x'^2+y'^2)^{3/2}

    = 4|c|^2 /(y^2+4c^2)^{3/2}.                     (1)

The unit normal (obtained by rotating the unit tangent through +\pi /2) is

    =( 2c + 3y^2/(4c) ,  -y^3/(4c^2) ).                          (4)

3. Elimination of y.  
Setting (x,y) = C(y) and eliminating y between (4) gives

 27 c y^2 = 4(x - 2c)^3,

which is ().  Each y corresponds to one point of H and vice-versa, hence H is the evolute of \Gamma .  By construction the circle with centre C(y) and radius \rho (y)=1/\kappa (y) satisfies third-order contact with \Gamma  at X(y) and is unique.

(a) Reality.  
Write (\star ) as Y^3 + A Y + B = 0 with

 A = 4c(2c - u), B = -8c^2 v.

The discriminant of a depressed cubic is

 D = -4A^3 - 27B^2 = -64 c^3 \Delta (P).                     (5)

Because c<0, the factor -64 c^3 is positive, hence sign(D)=sign(\Delta ).  
For a monic cubic:

 D>0  \Leftrightarrow  three distinct real roots,  
 D=0  \Leftrightarrow  at least two coincident real roots,

This gives the announced classification in terms of \Delta (P).

Let y_1,y_2,y_3 be the roots of (\star ).

 y_1+y_2+y_3 = 0,

 y_1y_2y_3 = -B.                                      (6)

Put x_i = y_i^2/(4c).  As in the original computation

 det(1,x_i,y_i) = -(y_1-y_2)(y_2-y_3)(y_3-y_1)/(4c).    (7)

Hence

        = D /(64 c^2)  (using (5))                  (8)
        = - c \Delta (P).                                (\ddagger )

Because -c > 0, \Delta (P)=0 \Leftrightarrow  A(P)=0, so the three feet are collinear
exactly when P lies on the evolute H.

(iv) The projective dual (normal) curve \Gamma *  

1. Plucker coordinates of a normal.  
The affine normal at X(y) has equation

 (y - y) = -y/(2c) (x - y^2/4c)
 \Leftrightarrow  (y/(2c)) x - y + (-y - y^3/(8c^2)) = 0.

Up to scale the Plucker coordinates are therefore  

 [s : t : u] = [ y : 2c : -2c y - y^3/(4c) ].          (9)

2. Eliminating y gives

 c s^3 + 2c s t^2 + u t^2 = 0,                          (\Delta )

an irreducible rational cubic with a unique cusp at [0:0:1].  
Hence \Gamma * is rational and cuspidal; the map y \mapsto  (9) has degree three, so
a generic line of \Gamma * is normal to \Gamma  at three points.

(v) Analytic stability under small perturbations  

Let F_\varepsilon (x,y)=y^2-4c x-\varepsilon  f(x).  A point (x,y) lies on \Gamma _\varepsilon \Leftrightarrow F_\varepsilon =0, and
\nabla F_\varepsilon =(-4c-\varepsilon  f'(x), 2y).
Repeating the calculation in (i) with these quantities produces

 y^3 + 4c(2c - u) y - 8c^2 v + \varepsilon  G_1(y;P) + \varepsilon ^2 G_2(y;P,\varepsilon ) = 0,   (\star _\varepsilon )

where G_1 is a polynomial of degree \leq 2 in y whose coefficients are
real-analytic in P and depend on f,f',f''.  The discriminant of (\star _\varepsilon )
is an analytic function of \varepsilon :

 Disc_\varepsilon (P) = \Delta (P) + \varepsilon  g(P) + O(\varepsilon ^2).                (10)

A direct differentiation of the classical discriminant formula yields

(a) If \Delta (P)\neq 0.  
Then Disc_\varepsilon (P)=\Delta (P)+O(\varepsilon ) never vanishes for |\varepsilon |<\varepsilon _0 small, so the
three normals remain simple; their real/complex nature does not change.

(b) If \Delta (P)=0 and P\neq (2c,0).  
Now Disc_\varepsilon (P)=\varepsilon  g(P)+O(\varepsilon ^2).  For g(P)\neq 0 the sign of \varepsilon  g(P) decides:

 \varepsilon  g(P) > 0 \Leftrightarrow  Disc_\varepsilon  > 0 \Leftrightarrow  three distinct real normals,

(c) The cusp P_0=(2c,0).  
Here y=0 is a triple root of the unperturbed cubic; a short computation
shows Disc_\varepsilon (P_0)=O(\varepsilon ^2).  Under a generic perturbation \Gamma  and its evolute
split into a 1+2 configuration, modelling the A_2-singularity.
